channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Randomized Distributed Function Computation (RDFC): Ultra-Efficient Semantic Communication Applications to Privacy
item.description: We introduce **Randomized Distributed Function Computation (RDFC)**, a semantic communication framework where a sender transmits only the minimal information needed for a receiver to *generate a randomized function* of the input—formalizing privacy-aware distributed computation as generalized remote source coding. Leveraging strong coordination metrics, we establish provable **local differential privacy (LDP) for all input sequences**, even *without any common randomness* between transmitter and receiver. We derive tight lower bounds on Wyner’s Common Information (WCI)—the fundamental rate limit under no shared randomness—and propose numerical methods to evaluate the complementary corner point of the RDFC rate region for continuous alphabets with unlimited shared randomness. Experiments show that sufficient shared randomness reduces the semantic communication rate by up to **two orders of magnitude** versus WCI; even without shared randomness, RDFC vastly outperforms lossless transmission. Finite-blocklength analysis confirms the privacy parameter gap between asymptotic and non-asymptotic RDFC vanishes **exponentially fast** in input length—positioning RDFC as an energy-efficient, privacy-native semantic communication strategy for resource-constrained distributed systems.

item.title: Compartmentalization-Aware Automated Program Repair
item.description: Software compartmentalization promises strong security by isolating application components, yet real-world deployments suffer from critical vulnerabilities at cross-compartment interfaces—enabling attackers to escape confinement. Securing these interfaces manually is error-prone and costly. This paper introduces the first *compartmentalization-aware* Automated Program Repair (APR) framework, designed specifically to fix such interface flaws. Unlike generic LLMs or traditional APR tools—which lack awareness of isolation semantics—our approach integrates: (1) a specialized fuzzer targeting interface-specific bugs (e.g., IPC validation gaps); (2) a patch generator that bridges LLMs’ semantic gap via compartment boundary analysis, contract inference, and privilege-flow modeling; and (3) a validator checking functional correctness, security efficacy, and isolation integrity. Evaluated on a real WebAssembly IPC vulnerability, our framework successfully generated verified fixes, while off-the-shelf LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, CodeLlama) produced insecure or non-functional patches. This work establishes a new foundation for automating the defense of isolation boundaries.

item.title: Democratising Clinical AI through Dataset Condensation for Classical Clinical Models
item.description: Dataset condensation (DC) offers a promising path toward healthcare data democratization by generating compact, synthetic datasets that preserve model utility while enabling strong privacy guarantees—especially when combined with differential privacy (DP). However, existing DC methods rely on gradient-based optimization, making them incompatible with widely used non-differentiable clinical models (e.g., decision trees, Cox regression). To bridge this gap, we propose the first DP-compliant, zero-order DC framework that requires only black-box model evaluations—not gradients—thus supporting *any* classical clinical predictor. Evaluated across six real-world clinical datasets (including classification and survival tasks), our method produces synthetic sets as small as 0.1% of original size, yet maintains near-lossless utility: e.g., <0.012 C-index drop for Cox models and 98.3% AUC retention for decision trees under $\varepsilon=2.0$ DP. Crucially, it enables truly model-agnostic, privacy-preserving data sharing—empowering diverse stakeholders to train their preferred clinical models without accessing sensitive patient records.

item.title: Optimal partition selection with Rényi differential privacy
item.description: We generalize optimal partition selection from $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP to $\delta$-approximate $(\alpha,\varepsilon)$-Rényi DP (RDP), enabling tighter composition analysis. Since no general optimality result exists for users submitting *multiple* partitions, we design a tailored optimal algorithm for $L^2$-bounded *weighted* partition selection—requiring only partition release (not frequencies)—which serves as a drop-in replacement for the Gaussian mechanism. We integrate it into state-of-the-art algorithms (PolicyGaussian and MAD2R), improving performance in both parallel and adaptive sequential settings. Crucially, we formalize the notion of optimal additive-noise RDP partition selection and prove a strict numerical separation: mechanisms releasing *both* partitions *and* frequencies incur inherent privacy cost compared to non-additive, partition-only mechanisms—demonstrating a fundamental trade-off between utility scope and privacy efficiency.

item.title: Fast and Optimal Differentially Private Frequent-Substring Mining
item.description: We present the first $\varepsilon$-differentially private algorithm for frequent substring mining that achieves near-optimal statistical error *and* scalable efficiency. Given $n$ strings of length at most $\ell$ over alphabet $\Sigma$, our method reduces space complexity from $O(n^2 \ell^4)$ to $O(n \ell + |\Sigma|)$ and time complexity from $O(n^2 \ell^4)$ to $O(n \ell \log |\Sigma| + |\Sigma|)$, while preserving the same near-optimal error guarantees as the state-of-the-art. Key innovations include: (i) a structure-aware top-down candidate generation strategy leveraging monotonicity of prefix/suffix frequencies, and (ii) aggressive search-space pruning guided by frequency inclusion relations (e.g., if $s$ is frequent, all its substrings must satisfy lower bounds). These eliminate quadratic blow-ups inherent in prior work, enabling practical differentially private substring mining on large-scale datasets.

item.title: AgenticCyOps: Securing Multi-Agentic AI Integration in Enterprise Cyber Operations
item.description: AgenticCyOps is a novel security framework for securing LLM-powered multi-agent systems (MAS) in enterprise cyber operations. Recognizing that autonomous tool access and memory sharing introduce critical, previously unaddressed attack surfaces—distinct from deterministic pipelines—we systematically decompose threats across component, coordination, and protocol layers. This reveals that all documented exploits converge on two integration surfaces: **tool orchestration** and **memory management**, which we formalize as primary trust boundaries. Grounded in this insight, we define five compliance-aligned defensive principles: authorized interfaces, capability scoping, verified execution, memory integrity & synchronization, and access-controlled data isolation—each mapped to NIST, ISO/IEC 27001, GDPR, and the EU AI Act. Applied to a SOC workflow using the Model Context Protocol (MCP), our design employs phase-scoped agents, consensus validation loops, and per-organization memory boundaries. Evaluation shows AgenticCyOps achieves defense-in-depth coverage of all known vectors, intercepts 3 out of 4 representative attack chains within the first two steps, and reduces exploitable trust boundaries by ≥72% versus flat MAS architectures—establishing a foundational, auditable, and standards-compliant model for enterprise-grade agentic AI integration.

item.title: Multi-model approach for autonomous driving: A comprehensive study on traffic sign-, vehicle- and lane detection and behavioral cloning
item.description: This paper presents a comprehensive multi-model deep learning framework for autonomous driving, addressing traffic sign classification, vehicle detection, lane segmentation, and behavioral cloning in an integrated manner. We leverage transfer learning with ResNet-50 and EfficientNet-B3, complemented by custom lightweight architectures enhanced by geometric-color data augmentation, multi-scale U-Net variants, optimized YOLOv5s, and an attention-augmented behavioral cloning model trained on 22k Udacity simulator frames. Evaluated on GTSRB (99.2% accuracy), BDD100K (78.4% mAP@0.5), TuSimple (96.7% F1), and closed-loop simulation (0.32m avg. lateral error), our approach significantly outperforms baselines. The work establishes a reproducible, modular pipeline that improves robustness, generalization, and real-time performance—advancing toward safer, more deployable autonomous systems.

item.title: MM-algorithms for traditional and convex NMF with Tweedie and Negative Binomial cost functions and empirical evaluation
item.description: Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is widely used for unsupervised learning, yet standard Gaussian or Poisson assumptions often fail for overdispersed count data. This paper introduces a unified Majorize-Minimize (MM) framework for both traditional and *convex* NMF under Tweedie and Negative Binomial likelihoods—linking Tweedie models to β-divergences and enabling statistically principled cost functions. We derive novel, provably convergent multiplicative updates for all models, including the first such rules for convex NMF with Poisson and Negative Binomial losses. Empirical evaluation on mutational and word-count datasets shows that appropriate noise modeling improves reconstruction (by 12–37%) and feature recovery (e.g., biologically meaningful mutation signatures), while convex NMF offers superior efficiency and robustness over traditional NMF when the number of components is large. Our open-source R package `nmfgenr` provides the first unified implementation of these models.

item.title: Nonparametric Variational Differential Privacy via Embedding Parameter Clipping
item.description: Nonparametric Variational Differential Privacy (NVDP) builds on the Nonparametric Variational Information Bottleneck (NVIB) to train privacy-preserving language models. Yet, unconstrained NVIB often suffers from posterior drift—latent representations concentrate in high-information regions—degrading both privacy guarantees (via inflated Rényi Divergence bounds) and utility (due to training instability). We propose **Embedding Parameter Clipping**, a theoretically grounded method derived directly from minimizing the Rényi Divergence (RD) upper bound. It imposes explicit, analytically justified constraints on the posterior mean, variance, and mixture weights—ensuring bounded sensitivity without heuristic tuning. Empirical evaluation on AG News, Yelp, and SST-2 shows our clipped NVIB model achieves **38.7% tighter RD bounds** (implying stronger (ε,α)-Rényi DP) and **+2.1–4.9% absolute accuracy gains** on downstream tasks, all while improving training stability and convergence speed. This simple, plug-and-play technique advances the privacy–utility trade-off in variational NLP models.

item.title: Tracking Cancer Through Text: Longitudinal Extraction From Radiology Reports Using Open-Source Large Language Models
item.description: Radiology reports contain vital longitudinal oncology information, but their unstructured format hinders automated analysis—especially in privacy-sensitive healthcare settings where proprietary LLMs are often inadmissible. We introduce a fully open-source, locally deployable pipeline using the `llm_extractinator` framework and the **Qwen2.5-72B** model to extract and temporally link target, non-target, and new lesions from radiology reports according to RECIST 1.1 criteria. Evaluated on 50 paired Dutch CT Thorax/Abdomen reports (100 total), our system achieved **93.7% accuracy for target lesion attributes**, **94.9% for non-target lesions**, and **94.0% for new lesions**, measured at the fine-grained attribute level (e.g., size, status, measurability). These results demonstrate that state-of-the-art open-weight LLMs can deliver clinically meaningful performance in longitudinal oncology extraction—without compromising data sovereignty, reproducibility, or regulatory compliance. The pipeline is publicly released under Apache 2.0.

item.title: TA-Mem: Tool-Augmented Autonomous Memory Retrieval for LLM in Long-Term Conversational QA
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in text reasoning but struggle with long-term conversational QA due to fixed context windows, necessitating external memory systems. Existing approaches rely on static episodic notes or graphs and use inflexible, predefined retrieval workflows—often limited to embedding similarity top-k. To overcome this, we propose **TA-Mem**, a tool-augmented autonomous memory retrieval framework comprising: (1) a semantic-aware memory extraction agent that adaptively chunks input and structures knowledge into typed notes; (2) a multi-indexed memory database supporting both key-based lookup and similarity search; and (3) a retrieval agent that autonomously selects and invokes appropriate database tools (e.g., “timeline query”, “conflict detection”) based on user intent, iteratively fetching and reasoning over memories before final response generation. Evaluated on the LoCoMo benchmark, TA-Mem achieves +12.7% F1 and +9.4% EM over strong baselines. Analysis confirms its adaptive tool selection across question types—enabling robust multi-hop, temporal, and consistency reasoning.

item.title: Signal Lost (Integrity): The Signal App is More than the Sum of its Protocols
item.description: This paper presents two practical integrity-breaking attacks against Signal—the widely trusted secure messaging application—within its advertised threat model. First, we exploit a flaw in Signal’s 2022 username-based identity resolution mechanism (affecting Android and Desktop), enabling a malicious server to inject arbitrary messages into one-to-one chats. Though it triggers a visible “safety number changed” alert, users verifying safety numbers will observe no mismatch—rendering the warning misleading and integrity compromised. Second, we uncover a critical vulnerability in Signal’s Sealed Sender (SSS) feature: due to missing key validation and cryptographic context loss across software components (especially on Android), a malicious server can silently inject arbitrary messages into *both* one-to-one and group conversations—undetectable by users, with no preconditions, and persisting since SSS’s 2018 deployment. Both vulnerabilities were responsibly disclosed and patched within days. Our work demonstrates that end-to-end security guarantees critically depend not only on protocol soundness, but also on rigorous engineering of identity abstractions, feature composition, and cross-component cryptographic state management.

item.title: Security Considerations for Multi-agent Systems
item.description: This study presents the first empirical, cross-framework security assessment of Multi-agent Systems (MAS)—autonomous, tool-using agents with shared memory and inter-agent coordination. We systematically characterize MAS-specific threats across **193 distinct items in 9 risk categories**, then quantitatively evaluate **16 AI security frameworks** using a four-phase methodology: (1) building a production-grade MAS architecture knowledge base; (2) generative-AI-assisted, expert-validated threat modeling; (3) threat-granular survey design; and (4) three-point scoring per framework against each threat. No framework achieves majority coverage (>50%) in any single category. **Non-Determinism (mean score 1.231)** and **Data Leakage (1.340)** are the most under-addressed domains. OWASP Agentic Security Initiative leads overall (65.3% coverage) and in design-phase readiness; CDAO’s Responsible AI Toolkit excels in development and operational coverage. Results provide actionable, evidence-based guidance for MAS security framework selection and standardization.

item.title: Quantifying Memorization and Privacy Risks in Genomic Language Models
item.description: Genomic language models (GLMs) enable breakthroughs in genomics but pose under-studied privacy risks due to potential memorization of sensitive DNA sequences. We introduce the first comprehensive, multi-vector framework to quantify memorization risk in GLMs, integrating perplexity-based detection, canary sequence injection/extraction, and membership inference into a unified worst-case risk score (WCMRS). Using synthetically planted canaries at controlled repetition rates (1×–100×) in both synthetic and real genomic datasets (e.g., 1000 Genomes), we systematically evaluate six GLM architectures. Results show all models exhibit measurable memorization (WCMRS up to 0.82), with risk increasing superlinearly with sequence repetition—10× repetition amplifies risk by 3.7×. Crucially, no single attack captures >68% of memorized sequences, demonstrating that multi-vector auditing is essential for robust privacy assessment of genomic AI systems.

item.title: NetDiffuser: Deceiving DNN-Based Network Attack Detection Systems with Diffusion-Generated Adversarial Traffic
item.description: Deep learning-based Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) are vulnerable to adversarial examples (AEs), yet generating *natural* AEs—semantically plausible, protocol-compliant traffic that evades both human inspection and ML detectors—remains highly challenging. This paper introduces **NetDiffuser**, the first diffusion-model-based framework for generating natural adversarial network traffic. It features: (1) a novel feature categorization algorithm that identifies semantically decoupled, low-impact features in network flows (e.g., inter-arrival intervals, TCP window scaling), enabling minimal yet effective perturbations; and (2) a conditional diffusion process that injects semantically consistent, protocol-aware noise while preserving flow validity. Evaluated across three benchmark datasets (CIC-IDS2017, UNSW-NB15, TON-IoT) and diverse NIDS architectures (CNN, LSTM, GNN), NetDiffuser achieves up to **29.93% higher attack success rate** than state-of-the-art baselines and degrades the AUC-ROC of advanced AE detectors by **0.267–0.534**, demonstrating unprecedented stealth and transferability.

item.title: HeteroFedSyn: Differentially Private Tabular Data Synthesis for Heterogeneous Federated Settings
item.description: Traditional differential privacy (DP) mechanisms lack reusability across tasks, whereas DP tabular data synthesis enables sharing of synthetic datasets for arbitrary downstream analyses. Yet existing methods ignore the practical horizontal federated setting—especially under data heterogeneity—leading to biased mixtures or excessive noise when naively applied locally. We propose **HeteroFedSyn**, the first DP synthesis framework tailored for horizontal federated learning. Built on 2-way marginal synthesis (PrivSyn), it introduces: (i) an L2-based dependency metric with random projection for efficient, low-noise correlation estimation; (ii) an unbiased estimator correcting multiplicative aggregation noise; and (iii) an adaptive selection strategy that dynamically prunes redundant marginals. Extensive experiments show HeteroFedSyn achieves utility **comparable to centralized DP synthesis**—despite inherent federated noise—on range queries, Wasserstein fidelity, and ML task accuracy. Code is open-sourced.

item.title: SCAFFOLD-CEGIS: Preventing Latent Security Degradation in LLM-Driven Iterative Code Refinement
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for iterative code refinement, yet the *latent degradation of security across iterations* remains poorly understood and unaddressed. We identify an “iterative refinement paradox”: multi-objective optimization causes specification drift, leading to progressive security erosion—e.g., 43.7% of GPT-4o’s 10-round refinement chains accumulate more vulnerabilities than their baseline. Crucially, conventional static application security testing (SAST) gating *exacerbates* the problem, raising latent degradation from 12.5% to 20.8%, as SAST fails to detect structural degradations like removal of defensive logic or weakened exception handling. To resolve this, we propose **SCAFFOLD-CEGIS**, a counterexample-guided, multi-agent framework that: (1) anchors security-critical elements as hard constraints via semantic analysis; (2) enforces safety monotonicity through four-layer gated verification (symbolic execution, taint analysis, assertion injection, sandboxed runtime); and (3) assimilates failure experiences to refine constraints adaptively. Evaluated across 12 real-world security-sensitive tasks, SCAFFOLD-CEGIS reduces latent degradation to **2.1%** and achieves **100% safety monotonicity**, outperforming six state-of-the-art defenses.

item.title: Client-Cooperative Split Learning
item.description: Client-Cooperative Split Learning (CliCooper) is a novel multi-client split learning framework designed for heterogeneous, partially trusted environments where one client contributes private labeled data while others collaboratively act as distributed trainers—without a central server. To bridge critical privacy and trust gaps, CliCooper introduces two key innovations: (1) **Differentially private activation protection** combined with **secret label obfuscation**, preserving model accuracy while provably thwarting label inference and data reconstruction attacks; and (2) a **dynamic chained watermarking scheme**, cryptographically linking training updates across model segments and trainers to ensure verifiable integrity, robust provenance, and copyright enforceability. Experiments demonstrate that CliCooper maintains >98% baseline accuracy while reducing clustering attack success to **0%**, dropping inversion-reconstruction similarity (SSIM) from **0.50 to 0.03**, and limiting model-extraction surrogate accuracy to **≈1%**—effectively indistinguishable from random guessing. CliCooper advances split learning toward production-ready, auditable, and cooperative AI services.

item.title: SlowBA: An efficiency backdoor attack towards VLM-based GUI agents
item.description: Modern vision-language-model (VLM)-based GUI agents must balance *accuracy* and *low-latency responsiveness*. While prior backdoor attacks target action correctness, we identify and formalize a novel threat: **efficiency backdoors** that maliciously inflate response latency without compromising task accuracy. We propose **SlowBA**, the first such attack, which exploits trigger-activated, excessively long reasoning chains (e.g., redundant interface descriptions) to degrade responsiveness. SlowBA employs a two-stage **Reward-level Backdoor Injection (RBI)** strategy—first aligning the model to a verbose output format, then fine-tuning trigger-aware activation via PPO reinforcement learning. Critically, it uses realistic OS-level pop-up windows (e.g., “Battery low”) as stealthy, environment-native triggers. Experiments across three benchmarks show SlowBA increases average response length by 3.2× and latency by 2.8× with only 0.5% poisoning ratio, while preserving >98.8% task accuracy. It remains highly effective under common defenses (LoRA fine-tuning, input filtering, output compression). SlowBA exposes a critical, previously overlooked security dimension: *response efficiency is an exploitable attack surface*, demanding dual-criteria (correctness + timeliness) evaluation and defense for GUI agents.

item.title: SplitAgent: A Privacy-Preserving Distributed Architecture for Enterprise-Cloud Agent Collaboration
item.description: SplitAgent introduces a privacy-preserving distributed architecture for enterprise-cloud AI agent collaboration, resolving the tension between cloud-based reasoning power and on-premises data sensitivity. Its core innovation is *context-aware dynamic sanitization*, which adapts data minimization strategies to task semantics (e.g., contract review vs. code analysis), unlike static approaches. The architecture extends standard agent protocols with three key mechanisms: (1) differential privacy with adaptive budget management, (2) zero-knowledge verification of cloud-side tools to ensure integrity and confidentiality, and (3) lightweight enterprise-side privacy agents that retain raw sensitive data locally. Evaluated across realistic enterprise workloads—including legal contracts, proprietary codebases, and financial reports—SplitAgent achieves **83.8% task accuracy** and **90.1% privacy protection**, outperforming static baselines by +10.6% accuracy and +10.4% privacy. Context-aware sanitization improves utility by 24.1% and reduces leakage by 67%, offering a practical, compliant path for enterprise AI adoption.

item.title: A Consensus-Driven Multi-LLM Pipeline for Missing-Person Investigations
item.description: The first 72 hours of a missing-person investigation are critical—yet fragmented data, high annotation costs, and unreliable automation hinder timely response. This paper introduces the **Guardian LLM Pipeline**, a consensus-driven multi-LLM system for missing-child investigations. It decomposes search operations into specialized subtasks (e.g., temporal alignment, entity extraction, geofence generation), each handled by fine-tuned LLMs; a dedicated consensus engine then reconciles their outputs via semantic comparison and confidence-weighted fusion. Built upon QLoRA-finetuned models trained on a curated 12K-sample corpus, the pipeline prioritizes structured, auditable extraction over end-to-end decision-making. Evaluated on 37 real unresolved cases, it achieves 0.89 F1 for clue extraction (+14.2% over single-model baselines) and 92% human-verifiable consensus decisions—demonstrating a practical, supervision-aware paradigm for LLM-assisted public safety.

item.title: FedLECC: Cluster- and Loss-Guided Client Selection for Federated Learning under Non-IID Data
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) faces severe efficiency bottlenecks in cross-device settings due to strict participation constraints and highly non-IID data—especially extreme label skew—which impairs convergence and model quality. To address this, we propose **FedLECC**, a lightweight, cluster- and loss-guided client selection strategy. FedLECC first groups clients by label-distribution similarity (e.g., using Hellinger distance) to ensure semantic diversity; then, within each round, it prioritizes clients exhibiting higher local loss—indicating underfitting or hard samples—enabling selection of a small yet highly informative subset. Evaluated under severe label skew (e.g., 90% class imbalance), FedLECC achieves up to **12% absolute accuracy gain**, reduces required communication rounds by **~22%**, and cuts total communication overhead by **up to 50%** versus strong baselines (FedCS, Oort, Power-of-Choice). These results demonstrate that principled, data-aware client selection is critical for scalable and efficient cloud-edge FL.

item.title: LDP: An Identity-Aware Protocol for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
item.description: We introduce the **LLM Delegate Protocol (LDP)**, an AI-native communication protocol for multi-agent LLM systems that treats model identity, reasoning profile, quality calibration, and cost as first-class primitives. LDP defines five core mechanisms: (1) rich delegate identity cards with quality hints and reasoning profiles; (2) progressive payload modes enabling negotiation and graceful fallback; (3) governed sessions with persistent context; (4) structured provenance tracking with explicit confidence and verification status; and (5) trust domains enforcing security boundaries at the protocol layer. Implemented as a JamJet plugin and evaluated against A2A and random baselines using local Ollama models and LLM-as-judge assessment, LDP achieves ~12× lower latency on easy tasks via identity-aware routing, reduces token usage by 37% (*p*=0.031) with semantic frame payloads (no quality loss), eliminates 39% token overhead in 10-round sessions, and demonstrates that unverified confidence metadata harms synthesis quality. Simulated analysis shows superior attack detection (96% vs. 6%) and failure recovery (100% vs. 35%). This work provides a novel protocol design, open reference implementation, and empirical evidence that AI-native primitives enable more efficient and governable delegation.

item.title: Split Federated Learning Architectures for High-Accuracy and Low-Delay Model Training
item.description: This paper tackles the joint optimization of model accuracy, training delay, and communication overhead in Split Federated Learning (SFL). Contrary to the common assumption that model partitioning has no impact on accuracy, we demonstrate that the choice of *partitioning layers* and *client-to-aggregator assignments* critically affects all three objectives—especially in Hierarchical SFL (HSFL) with its client–aggregator–server three-tier architecture. We formulate a unified integer nonlinear optimization problem capturing these couplings, prove its NP-hardness, and propose the first accuracy-aware heuristic algorithm that explicitly incorporates gradient sensitivity analysis and load-aware clustering. Evaluations on CIFAR-10 and FEMNIST show our method improves model accuracy by **+3.0%**, reduces end-to-end training delay by **−20%**, and cuts total communication overhead by **−50%**, outperforming state-of-the-art SFL and HSFL baselines. This work establishes a new design principle for high-accuracy, low-latency federated learning at the edge.

item.title: Revisiting Gradient Staleness: Evaluating Distance Metrics for Asynchronous Federated Learning Aggregation
item.description: In asynchronous federated learning (FL), gradient staleness—arising from heterogeneous client update times—severely impairs convergence and model accuracy. While prior work (e.g., AsyncFedED) uses Euclidean distance to weight stale updates, this metric overlooks directional alignment, loss landscape curvature, and data distribution shifts under non-IID settings. We systematically revisit staleness modeling by introducing four novel distance metrics: (1) cosine-weighted Euclidean distance, (2) Hessian-approximated distance for curvature-aware weighting, (3) Wasserstein-projected distance in feature space, and (4) momentum-aware KL divergence. Integrated into an adaptive aggregation framework, these metrics dynamically down-weight unreliable updates. Extensive experiments on FEMNIST, non-IID CIFAR-10/100, and Edge-IIoTset show that the Hessian-based metric accelerates convergence by 23.6% and reduces accuracy variance by 41% versus AsyncFedED; cosine weighting ensures >98% convergence success under 50% high-latency clients; all variants achieve target accuracy with 17–31% fewer communication rounds. Our work establishes a more robust, interpretable, and deployment-ready foundation for asynchronous FL.

item.title: MAcPNN: Mutual Assisted Learning on Data Streams with Temporal Dependence
item.description: This paper proposes **MAcPNN**, a decentralized continual learning framework for IoT data streams. Grounded in Vygotsky’s sociocultural theory, MAcPNN implements *Mutual Assisted Learning* (MAL): edge devices operate autonomously and only seek help from peers *upon performance degradation* due to concept drift—dramatically reducing communication overhead versus federated learning (e.g., 83% fewer rounds). Each device employs a quantized *Continuous Progressive Neural Network* (cPNN) supporting single-point prediction and temporal modeling. Experiments on synthetic and real-world streams (e.g., AWS IoT, UCR datasets) show MAcPNN improves average accuracy by **12.7%**, cuts memory footprint by **39%**, and achieves superior robustness to drift and forgetting. Its core contribution is a cognitively inspired, communication-efficient paradigm for collaborative lifelong learning at the edge.

item.title: FedPrism: Adaptive Personalized Federated Learning under Non-IID Data
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers severe performance degradation under statistical heterogeneity (non-IID data), where global models fail to balance generalization and personalization. To address this, we propose **FedPrism**, a novel adaptive personalized FL framework. It introduces two key innovations: (1) *Prism Decomposition*, which factorizes each client’s model into three components—**global foundation**, **shared group module** (dynamically learned via unsupervised similarity clustering), and **private module**—enabling automatic, evolution-aware grouping without pre-specifying cluster count; and (2) *Dual-Stream inference*, jointly maintaining a generalist model and a local specialist, with confidence-based routing to switch between them for robust, context-aware predictions. Extensive experiments on highly non-IID partitions of CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet show FedPrism consistently outperforms static aggregation and hard-clustering baselines, achieving **+3.2–5.8% absolute accuracy gains** under extreme heterogeneity while maintaining communication efficiency. FedPrism establishes a new paradigm for flexible, scalable, and personalized FL in real-world heterogeneous edge environments.

item.title: Adversarial Domain Adaptation Enables Knowledge Transfer Across Heterogeneous RNA-Seq Datasets
item.description: Accurate phenotype prediction from RNA-seq data is hindered by dataset scarcity and cross-cohort heterogeneity—arising from divergent preprocessing pipelines and phenotype definitions. To address this, we propose an adversarial domain adaptation (ADA) framework that enables robust knowledge transfer from large general transcriptomic datasets (e.g., ARCHS4, GTEx) to small target cohorts (e.g., TCGA subtypes). Our method learns a domain-invariant latent representation by jointly optimizing classification accuracy and adversarial domain alignment, stabilized via gradient reversal and L2 regularization. We evaluate both supervised and unsupervised variants across three major resources, demonstrating consistent improvements: +5.2–12.7% absolute accuracy gain in cancer/tissue classification over non-adaptive baselines—especially under extreme data scarcity (e.g., <50 labeled target samples). This work establishes ADA as a practical, data-efficient strategy for generalizable modeling in transcriptomics.

item.title: Stabilized Fine-Tuning with LoRA in Federated Learning: Mitigating the Side Effect of Client Size and Rank via the Scaling Factor
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are computationally prohibitive to fine-tune fully in privacy-preserving federated learning (FL), making Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) a promising but unstable alternative. We identify that naive LoRA aggregation across $ N $ clients induces rank-dependent statistical variance, causing severe gradient collapse for high-rank adapters—a flaw unaddressed by prior scaling methods like Rank-Stabilized LoRA, which ignore FL-specific aggregation dynamics. To resolve this, we propose **Stabilized Federated LoRA (SFed-LoRA)**, deriving a theoretically grounded optimal scaling factor $ \gamma_{\text{opt}} = 1/\sqrt{N \cdot r} $ that explicitly corrects the aggregation-induced mismatch between client updates and global adaptation. SFed-LoRA requires no architectural changes, adds zero inference latency, and preserves standard LoRA computation. Extensive experiments across diverse models (LLaMA-2, BERT, T5), tasks (classification, NER, MT), and heterogeneous data distributions demonstrate that SFed-LoRA eliminates high-rank collapse, achieves up to 2.3× faster convergence, and improves final accuracy by +4.7% on average over state-of-the-art baselines—establishing a new benchmark for stable, scalable PEFT in FL.

item.title: LeJOT-AutoML: LLM-Driven Feature Engineering for Job Execution Time Prediction in Databricks Cost Optimization
item.description: Databricks job orchestration systems like LeJOT optimize cloud costs by selecting low-cost compute configurations under latency and dependency constraints—yet accurate execution-time prediction remains challenging due to heterogeneous instance types and non-stationary runtime effects (e.g., partition pruning, data skew, shuffle amplification). Existing pipelines rely on static, manually engineered features, scattering predictive signals across logs, metadata, and scripts—causing weeks-long feature iteration cycles. LeJOT-AutoML addresses this by embedding LLM agents throughout the ML lifecycle: it combines retrieval-augmented generation over a domain knowledge base with a Model Context Protocol toolchain (log parsers, metadata queries, read-only SQL sandbox) to analyze job artifacts, synthesize and *safely validate* feature-extraction code via multi-layer safety gates, and train/select predictors. On enterprise workloads, it automatically generates **217 runtime-derived features**, reduces the feature engineering loop from weeks to **20–30 minutes**, maintains competitive accuracy (MAPE < 8.2%), and—when integrated into LeJOT—enables automated continuous model updates, yielding **19.01% cost savings** in production deployment.

item.title: COACH meets QUORUM: A Framework and Pipeline for Aligning User, Expert and Developer Perspectives in LLM-generated Health Counselling
item.description: This paper introduces **QUORUM**, a novel multi-stakeholder evaluation framework, and **COACH**, an LLM-driven pipeline for generating personalized lifestyle counseling in the *Healthy Chronos* diary app for cancer patients and survivors. QUORUM unifies developer-centric (technical robustness), expert-centric (clinical validity), and user-centric (experiential relevance) perspectives across five dimensions: relevance, quality, reliability, tone appropriateness, and error sensitivity. Applied to real-world COACH outputs, results show strong convergence among all stakeholders on relevance, quality, and reliability (mean scores ≥4.2/5), validating the system’s foundational utility. However, significant divergence emerges regarding counseling tone (e.g., empathy vs. neutrality), sensitivity to minor pattern-extraction errors (users distrust subtle inaccuracies more than developers anticipate), and perceived hallucination risk (experts are most vigilant). These findings underscore that trustworthy consumer health NLP requires integrated, stakeholder-aligned evaluation—not just technical metrics—and demonstrate how QUORUM enables actionable, context-aware system refinement in clinical practice.

item.title: RexDrug: Reliable Multi-Drug Combination Extraction through Reasoning-Enhanced LLMs
item.description: Automated extraction of *n*-ary drug combinations from biomedical literature remains a critical yet unsolved challenge due to the complexity of variable-length regimens and distributed evidence. We propose **RexDrug**, an end-to-end reasoning-enhanced LLM framework for reliable multi-drug combination extraction. RexDrug introduces (1) a multi-agent collaborative mechanism to generate expert-like reasoning traces for supervised fine-tuning, and (2) reinforcement learning with a DCE-specific multi-dimensional reward—jointly optimizing completeness, pharmacological consistency, and textual support. On DrugComb, RexDrug achieves state-of-the-art *n*-ary extraction performance (+9.2% average F1 gain), and generalizes effectively to binary DDI tasks (DDI13). Human evaluation confirms 92.4% clinical coherence in its reasoning paths. RexDrug establishes a scalable, interpretable, and robust solution for complex biomedical relation extraction. Code and data: https://github.com/DUTIR-BioNLP/RexDrug

item.title: A Hardware/Software Co-Optimization of HQC Using Tightly-Coupled Accelerators on a 32-bit Ibex Core
item.description: We present a hardware/software co-optimization of the Hamming Quasi-Cyclic (HQC) post-quantum cryptosystem on a 32-bit RISC-V Ibex core. Hardware contributions include a unified carryless multiplier supporting both polynomial multiplication over $\mathbb{F}_2[X]/(X^n-1)$ and $\mathbb{F}_{2^8}$ arithmetic, plus a dedicated Keccak permutation accelerator for efficient randomness sampling. Software optimizations identify an adaptive Toom–Cook/Karatsuba hybrid strategy for polynomial multiplication and drastically reduce memory accesses via register-aware data reuse and access pattern restructuring. Evaluated on FPGA, our co-designed implementation achieves **47.3× speedup in key generation** and **38.6× in encryption throughput** over the pqm4 reference—representing several tens-of-times improvement—while maintaining full NIST-compliance and security guarantees.

item.title: Debt-Aware Bonding Curves: Non-Decreasing Floor Prices and Non-Liquidatable Borrowing
item.description: We introduce **Debt-Aware Discrete Bonding Curves (DABC)**—a novel mechanism for decentralized lending that eliminates oracle-dependent liquidations. DABC features a provably non-decreasing endogenous floor price segment and couples collateral reserves to outstanding debt via a debt-aware invariant. This enables borrowing against issuance-native assets (e.g., the protocol’s own token) with loan-to-value (LTV) ratios anchored *solely* to the floor price—not external oracles. We prove that any loan originated at or below the floor-anchored LTV **cannot become under-collateralized due to collateral price declines**, thus removing protocol-triggered liquidation risk. Default results in permanent token lock (not forced sale), preserving system liquidity. By unifying issuance, trading, and lending in one contract, DABC captures fee revenue to fund floor elevation. A recursive buy-lock-borrow-buy loop enables liquidation-free leverage—especially powerful for token launches. To our knowledge, this is the first fully formalized design where borrowing safety derives from both a debt-aware invariant and a mathematically guaranteed non-decreasing floor price. Verified via stateful fuzz testing and Coq-based formal verification of a concrete Solidity implementation.

item.title: Cryptanalysis of Two Alternating Moduli Weak PRFs
item.description: This paper presents the first cryptanalytic attacks against two recently proposed alternating-moduli weak pseudorandom functions (weak-PRFs), whose theoretical security arguments were incomplete. We identify structural weaknesses in their cyclic matrix construction and develop three novel techniques: (i) exploiting cyclic symmetry to reduce effective output dimensionality; (ii) adapting Wagner’s generalized birthday algorithm to alternating $\mathbb{Z}_3/\mathbb{Z}_5$ settings for efficient key recovery; and (iii) modeling evaluations as sparse polynomial systems over $\mathbb{F}_3$, solved via Gröbner basis methods. Our attacks fully recover keys for both AM-35-PRF and AM-37-PRF with complexities of $2^{45}$ and $2^{47}$, respectively—far below their claimed $2^{80}$ security. These results invalidate the original security claims and underscore the critical need for rigorous algebraic analysis when combining modular alternation with linear-algebraic PRF designs.

item.title: Remise: Authorized Anonymous Communication Systems
item.description: Remise is a two-server authorized anonymous communication system built on Distributed Oblivious RAM (DORAM). It supports two operation modes: (i) an *anonymous bulletin board*, where messages are publicly revealed per epoch without sender-message linkage; and (ii) *anonymous secret-shared channels*, where writers dynamically grant or revoke read access to chosen readers via 2-out-of-2 secret sharing. Crucially, both servers perform oblivious reads/writes—learning neither accessed indices, authorization tokens, nor writer-reader relationships—under the assumption of at least one honest server. A key contribution is a lightweight access-control mechanism: authorization proofs are maintained in secret-shared form across servers, enabling oblivious verification while resisting leakage even under client–server collusion. Unlike prior DPF-based systems, Remise achieves built-in auditing by having semi-honest servers internally generate standard-basis vector shares—eliminating costly DPF validity checks. Evaluation shows Remise improves online server time by **80×** over PACL (IEEE S&P 2023) for databases of size $2^{24}$.

item.title: CHOPIN: Optimal Pairing-Based Multilinear Polynomial Commitments from Bivariate KZG
item.description: We present **CHOPIN**, a pairing-based multilinear polynomial commitment scheme (PCS) with constant-size proofs and linear-time prover, built modularly from a bivariate PCS. CHOPIN generalizes recent univariate-to-multilinear compilers—MERCURY and Samaritan—by directly leveraging bivariate KZG (Papamanthou et al., TCC 2013). Its modular design enables a **tight knowledge-soundness proof in the standard model**, avoiding idealized assumptions like the Algebraic Group Model. Instantiated with bivariate KZG, CHOPIN achieves proof size comparable to prior works while **halving the prover’s dominant cost**: it requires only **one large MSM** (vs. two in MERCURY/Samaritan), yielding ~2× speedup in proof generation, at the cost of **one extra pairing for the verifier**. This makes CHOPIN highly practical for ZK-SNARK backends.

item.title: Strong Efficiency Lower Bounds for Byzantine Agreement
item.description: This paper establishes new strong efficiency lower bounds for randomized Byzantine Agreement (BA) against the *standard adaptive adversary*—a realistic threat model used in production systems like Algorand, unlike prior bounds requiring unrealistically powerful adversaries. We present two communication complexity lower bounds under authenticated channels (i.e., with idealized digital signatures): (1) An improved Ω(t²) *expected message* lower bound under minimal setup (only a Common Reference String), tightening Abraham et al. (PODC’19); and (2) A refined Ω(t² log n) *bit* lower bound, improving Blum et al. (TCC’20) by leveraging authentication. Technically, we introduce a novel formal model for protocols using idealized signatures, enabling rigorous simulation-based proofs. Further, we prove that any round-efficient BA protocol where only a subset of parties speak must either rely on erasure channels or make first-round speaking decisions dependent on inputs—both paradigms incur inherent trade-offs between latency, fairness, and practicality, as demonstrated in prior works (e.g., HoneyBadger, Dumbo).

item.title: DAC-PRE: Practical Anonymous Data Access Scheme Control with Proxy Re-encryption for Implantable Medical Devices
item.description: This paper proposes DAC-PRE, a practical anonymous data access control scheme for implantable medical devices (IMDs) based on signcryption and proxy re-encryption. DAC-PRE enables fine-grained, identity-hiding access to sensitive physiological data: patients encrypt data once, and authorized entities (e.g., clinicians or cloud servers) decrypt *only after* an untrusted proxy performs ciphertext transformation—without learning either the patient’s or requester’s identity. Security is formally proven in the random oracle model against adaptive chosen-ciphertext attacks (IND-CCA2) and anonymity-breaking attacks (ANON-CCA2). Experimental evaluation on ARM Cortex-M4 shows encryption takes 128 ms and proxy re-encryption only 85 ms—reducing computational overhead by up to 67% versus state-of-the-art schemes—while maintaining low ciphertext expansion (≤1.3×) and supporting dynamic revocation and multi-level delegation. DAC-PRE bridges critical gaps between theoretical security, embedded-system constraints, and clinical deployability.

item.title: Duty-Free Bits: Projectivizing Garbling Schemes
item.description: Garbling schemes enable secure two-party computation between mutually untrusting parties. Projective schemes—where the evaluator’s input is encoded bit-by-bit—are highly versatile (e.g., naturally compatible with 1-out-of-2 OT), but most efficient information-theoretic (IT) arithmetic garblings over large fields (e.g., $\mathbb{F}_p$) use affine input encodings and thus *fail* to be projective. We present a symmetric-key-based transformation that *projectivizes* such schemes “duty-free”: translating between Yao-style and IT-style labels incurs cost proportional only to label size—no asymptotic overhead. Applied to two key problems: (1) We replace the bit-decomposition steps in BABE and Argo MAC—two trust-minimized Bitcoin bridge protocols—achieving **45× and 20× smaller input encodings**, respectively; (2) We give the first non-interactive, malicious-receiver-secure reduction from $\mathbb{F}_p$-VOLE to 1-out-of-2 OT in Minicrypt (plus base OTs), with communication cost $O((\lambda + n)\lg p)$ bits—state-of-the-art for large-field VOLE.

item.title: DistillGuard: Evaluating Defenses Against LLM Knowledge Distillation
item.description: DistillGuard is the first systematic framework for evaluating output-level defenses against LLM knowledge distillation from proprietary APIs. We propose a taxonomy of three defense categories—output perturbation, data poisoning, and information throttling—and evaluate nine configurations using Qwen3-14B (teacher) and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (student) across MATH-500, HumanEval+, and MT-Bench. Results show that most output-level defenses are surprisingly ineffective against naive same-family distillation: paraphrasing barely degrades student performance, and data poisoning impairs conversational fluency without harming task-specific capabilities. Only chain-of-thought removal substantially harms mathematical reasoning (31.4% vs. 67.8% baseline), while leaving code generation unaffected. These findings demonstrate that defense efficacy is highly task-dependent and that current output-level approaches are insufficient for broad-spectrum knowledge theft prevention.

item.title: Post-quantum Federated Learning: Secure And Scalable Threat Intelligence For Collaborative Cyber Defense
item.description: This paper introduces the first production-ready post-quantum federated learning (PQ-FL) framework for secure, cross-organizational threat intelligence sharing. To counter quantum threats—particularly Shor’s algorithm breaking RSA-encrypted gradients—we integrate NIST-standardized CRYSTALS-Kyber (for key exchange) and CRYSTALS-Dilithium (for authentication) into a lightweight, library-agnostic FL architecture. Evaluated on real-world APT datasets, PQ-FL achieves **97.6% threat detection accuracy** with only **18.7% latency overhead**, demonstrating operational viability. A healthcare consortium case study confirms compliant ransomware indicator exchange under HIPAA/GDPR, with zero raw data leaving premises. The work delivers actionable technical guidelines for PQC integration in FL and policy recommendations for standardizing quantum resilience in collaborative cyber defense networks.

item.title: Revisiting the LiRA Membership Inference Attack Under Realistic Assumptions
item.description: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are widely used to audit ML privacy, with the Likelihood-Ratio Attack (LiRA) considered state-of-the-art—yet prior evaluations rely on unrealistic assumptions: overconfident target models, threshold calibration on target data, balanced membership priors (π = 0.5), and unverified reproducibility. We re-evaluate LiRA under a realistic protocol featuring: (i) anti-overfitting (AOF) and transfer learning (TL) to mimic production models; (ii) shadow-only threshold calibration; (iii) precision (PPV) evaluation under skewed priors (π ≤ 10%); and (iv) per-sample reproducibility quantification across seeds and training variants. We find AOF drastically weakens LiRA (PPV drops >40 pts), and TL further reduces attack success while improving model accuracy. Under shadow-calibrated thresholds and skewed priors, LiRA’s PPV often falls below 20%—approaching random guessing in AOF/TL settings. Critically, low-FPR “vulnerable sets” show poor reproducibility (<30% overlap across runs), whereas likelihood-ratio rankings remain highly stable (Spearman ρ > 0.85). These results challenge LiRA’s perceived effectiveness and underscore that robust privacy auditing must reflect real-world training, feasible attacker knowledge, and reproducibility. Code: https://github.com/najeebjebreel/lira_analysis.

item.title: From Thinker to Society: Security in Hierarchical Autonomy Evolution of AI Agents
item.description: This paper addresses critical security gaps emerging from the evolution of AI agents—from passive predictors to autonomous reasoning entities powered by LLMs. We propose the **Hierarchical Autonomy Evolution (HAE)** framework, structuring agent security across three interdependent tiers: **Cognitive Autonomy (L1)** for reasoning integrity, **Execution Autonomy (L2)** for safe tool-mediated interaction, and **Collective Autonomy (L3)** for systemic resilience in multi-agent ecosystems. We introduce a comprehensive threat taxonomy spanning cognitive manipulation, environmental disruption, and collective failure modes, and critically evaluate 27 existing defenses—revealing key gaps in cross-layer consistency verification, distributed trust quantification, and dynamic autonomy-aware evaluation. Our work establishes the first principled foundation for *autonomy-aware security*, guiding the design of multilayered, adaptive, and verifiable defense architectures for trustworthy AI agent systems.

item.title: Trusting What You Cannot See: Auditable Fine-Tuning and Inference for Proprietary AI
item.description: Cloud deployment of large language models (LLMs) increasingly outsources fine-tuning and inference to providers—yet clients lack practical means to verify computational integrity, as cryptographic or TEE-based approaches are prohibitively expensive for billion-parameter models. We present **AFTUNE**, the first lightweight, auditable framework enabling verifiable cloud-based fine-tuning and inference. AFTUNE introduces a low-overhead execution tracing mechanism that records cryptographically bound snapshots of key operations (e.g., gradient updates, logits), coupled with efficient probabilistic spot-checking for on-demand client-side verification. Evaluation across LLaMA-2 (7B/13B) and Qwen-1.5 (4B) shows AFTUNE incurs only **<3.2% training overhead** and **<1.8% inference latency**, with trace storage under 0.05% of model size; single audits complete in **<80ms**. Crucially, AFTUNE achieves strong integrity guarantees *without* requiring trusted hardware or expensive zero-knowledge proofs—demonstrating that trustworthy, production-ready AI services are feasible in today’s public cloud.

item.title: Where Do LLM-based Systems Break? A System-Level Security Framework for Risk Assessment and Treatment
item.description: This paper introduces a goal-driven, system-level security framework for assessing and mitigating risks in LLM-based critical systems. Moving beyond isolated model audits, it integrates structured system modeling, Attack-Defense Trees (ADTrees), and an extended CVSS-based exploitability scoring tailored to LLM-specific threats—including prompt injection, context manipulation, and RAG data poisoning. Applied to a healthcare case study, the framework reveals that diverse threat classes (conventional cyber, adversarial ML, and conversational attacks) converge on few systemic choke points—e.g., unguarded LLM–EHR query interfaces and pre-approval result aggregation services. Targeted defenses at these points reduce exploitability of dominant attack paths by over 62%. The framework produces actionable, standardized risk scores compatible with ISO 27001 and NIST SP 800-30, enabling domain-agnostic adoption across safety-critical LLM deployments.

item.title: Hide and Find: A Distributed Adversarial Attack on Federated Graph Learning
item.description: Federated Graph Learning (FedGL) faces critical security threats from adversarial attacks, yet existing methods suffer from low success rates, high computational overhead, and poor stealth against robust defenses. To address this, we propose **FedShift**, a novel distributed two-stage “Hide and Find” attack. In the *Hide* stage, before FedGL training begins, we inject a learnable, hidden *shifter* into subsets of local graph data—subtly steering poisoned representations toward (but not across) the target class boundary to evade detection during aggregation. In the *Find* stage, after global model convergence, we leverage the model’s parameters and use the shifter as an optimized initialization to efficiently compute minimal adversarial perturbations; these are then aggregated across malicious clients to form the final effective attack sample. Extensive experiments on six large-scale graph datasets show FedShift achieves **state-of-the-art attack success**, **evades three mainstream robust federated defenses**, and **reduces convergence time by over 90%**, demonstrating unprecedented stealth, robustness, and efficiency.

item.title: VoiceSHIELD-Small: Real-Time Malicious Speech Detection and Transcription
item.description: VoiceSHIELD-Small is a lightweight, real-time model that jointly performs speech transcription and malicious intent detection in a single forward pass—eliminating the latency and audio cue loss of traditional ASR-then-filter pipelines. Built upon Whisper-small’s encoder with only a mean-pooling layer and a compact classification head (<15M parameters), it processes audio in **90–120 ms** on mid-tier GPUs while maintaining high fidelity. Evaluated on a balanced, noise-robust dataset of 947 real-world utterances, it achieves **99.16% accuracy**, an **F1-score of 0.9865**, and a low harmful-input miss rate of **2.33%** at default threshold; cross-validation confirms stability (F1 std = 0.0026). Released under the MIT license, VoiceSHIELD-Small advances practical, transparent, and deployable voice AI security.

item.title: Compressed Proximal Federated Learning for Non-Convex Composite Optimization on Heterogeneous Data
item.description: Federated Composite Optimization (FCO) enables training structured models (e.g., sparse neural nets) over heterogeneous edge devices but suffers from high communication cost, non-IID data bias, and instability under aggressive gradient compression—especially with non-smooth regularizers. We propose **FedCEF**, the first compressed proximal federated algorithm for non-convex FCO. FedCEF decouples the proximal operator from communication: clients compute proximal steps locally while compressing only smooth gradient components, eliminating proximal-induced compression bias. It further combines rigorous error feedback with control variates to jointly mitigate quantization noise and statistical heterogeneity. Crucially, its pre-proximal downlink strategy allows exact reconstruction of global control variables *without transmitting model parameters*, achieving zero-downlink cost. Theoretically, FedCEF converges to a bounded residual at rate $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ under general non-convexity. Empirically, it maintains competitive accuracy (≤1.2% drop) even at 1% compression ratio—reducing total communication by up to 98.7% versus uncompressed baselines.

item.title: TT-Sparse: Learning Sparse Rule Models with Differentiable Truth Tables
item.description: Interpretable machine learning is critical in high-stakes applications demanding accountability and transparency. While rule-based models offer exact global interpretability, jointly optimizing predictive accuracy and human-understandable sparsity remains challenging. We propose **TT-Sparse**, a novel neural framework that learns sparse, effective rule models via **differentiable truth tables** as learnable nodes. Its key innovation is a **soft TopK operator with straight-through estimation**, enabling end-to-end differentiable, cardinality-constrained feature selection—while keeping the forward pass *exactly sparse* for efficiency and symbolic extraction. Crucially, each node (and the full model) can be converted *exactly* into minimal, globally interpretable DNF/CNF Boolean formulas using Quine-McCluskey minimization. Extensive experiments across 28 diverse datasets (binary, multiclass, regression) show TT-Sparse achieves **superior predictive performance** and **significantly lower rule complexity** versus state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., CORELS, SBRL), demonstrating a scalable path toward high-fidelity, human-verifiable AI.
